title: FoMoE: Breaking the Full-Replica Barrier with a Federation of MoEs
summary: Pre-training Large Language Models (LLMs) typically demands large-scale infrastructure with tightly coupled hardware accelerators. While increasing model and dataset scale remains the dominant driver of performance, Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) architectures have recently achieved state-of-the-art results by decoupling parameter count from computational cost. This efficiency enables training massive models on constrained compute budgets, yet it typically requires the high-speed interconnects of a single datacenter. To overcome these physical limits, recent approaches such as DiLoCo and Photon use low-communication data-parallel methods to enable scaling across geographically distributed, weakly connected data centers. However, these methods suffer from a fundamental inefficiency: they require full model replicas at every site, which imposes prohibitive memory constraints and communication overheads. In this work, we introduce FoMoE, a system that breaks the full-replica paradigm by partitioning expert layers across workers. We demonstrate that FoMoE: (I) reduces communication costs by up to 1.42x over efficient baselines and 45.44x over DDP via partial expert replication in the studied regimes; (II) achieves empirical throughput speedups of up to 1.4x through a novel skip-token mechanism; and (III) shows stable routing in the trained proxy regimes and projects the communication/memory benefits to 100B-scale configurations through system modelling.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FoMoE**, a novel system designed to train massive **Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)** models across geographically distributed, weakly connected data centers.
- Breaks the **full-replica barrier** by partitioning expert layers across workers, addressing the memory and communication bottlenecks found in prior methods like DiLoCo.
- Features a **skip-token mechanism** and **partial expert replication** to optimize throughput and reduce the overhead of high-latency interconnects.
- Scales the benefits of distributed training to **100B-parameter configurations** while maintaining stable routing performance.
key_results: Reduces communication costs by 1.42x over optimized baselines.
Achieves 45.44x lower communication overhead compared to standard DDP.
Delivers up to 1.4x empirical throughput speedup via skip-token mechanism.
Maintains stable expert routing during training in proxy regimes.
Projects significant memory and bandwidth efficiency for 100B-scale models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FoMoE to enable training massive Mixture-of-Experts models across distributed data centers by partitioning expert layers.

title: Lifecycle-Aware Dynamic Analysis for Secure ML Model Execution
summary: The growing reliance on pre-trained Machine Learning (ML) models has introduced new attack surfaces. Recent vulnerabilities demonstrate that malicious behavior can be embedded within model artifacts, often bypassing existing defenses. Current model-scanning solutions primarily rely on static, format-specific rules or known attack signatures, which limit their ability to generalize across frameworks and to detect novel exploitation paths. In contrast, we propose a solution that focuses on the effects an attack has on the host system executing the model and builds on foundational intuitions about ML model execution. In particular, we observe that ML models operate within well-defined lifecycle phases and that, within each phase, interactions with the host system are highly structured and predictable. We translate these intuitions into Moat, a dynamic lifecycle-aware approach for securing ML model execution, and instantiate this design in Re-Moat, our reference implementation. We evaluate Re-Moat across multiple ML frameworks using 77,974 real-world model artifacts from the Hugging Face Hub, 31 Proofs-of-Concept (PoCs) from CVEs, and 334 models from a state-of-the-art dataset, and compare it against state-of-the-art model-scanning solutions. Our results show that our approach detects all evaluated attack classes while maintaining a close-to-zero false-positive rate, validating our intuitions and motivating dynamic analysis for securing ML model execution.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Moat**, a security framework utilizing **dynamic lifecycle-aware analysis** to monitor interactions between ML models and host systems.
- Shifts focus from static signature-based scanning to observing **runtime behaviors** across distinct execution phases like loading, initialization, and inference.
- Implements **Re-Moat**, a reference system designed to detect novel exploitation paths and malicious embedded artifacts across diverse ML frameworks.
- Establishes a defense against **supply chain attacks** by ensuring model-to-OS interactions remain within predictable, highly structured boundaries.
key_results: Detected 100% of evaluated attack classes across 31 CVE Proofs-of-Concept.
Maintained a near-zero false-positive rate during testing on 77,974 real-world Hugging Face models.
Demonstrated framework-agnostic detection capabilities, outperforming format-specific static scanners.
Identified malicious behaviors embedded in model artifacts that bypass traditional signature-based defenses.
Validated that ML execution phases are sufficiently structured to allow for precise behavioral anomaly detection.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Moat, a dynamic analysis framework that secures ML model execution by monitoring system-level lifecycle interactions.

title: FlowObject: Flow Steering for Bridging Generative Priors and Reconstruction Fidelity
summary: Recovering complete 3D representations of objects from few casual image captures remains a significant challenge. Recent 3D generative models, particularly those based on Flow-Matching (FM), can synthesize high-quality textured assets; however, they often suffer from ''synthetic bias'' where learned priors override observational evidence, alongside a lack of alignment with the observed instance. Conversely, optimization-based methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provide high fidelity on visible surfaces but fail to reason about unobserved geometry. In this paper, we present FlowObject, a framework that reformulates sparse-view 3D reconstruction as a training-free, guided inverse problem. Our approach applies a dual-space guidance strategy to steer the Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) trajectory of a flow-matching model, enabling the completion of unseen regions through learned generative priors while enforcing strict consistency with real-world observations. By integrating a 3DGS refinement stage, FlowObject further bridges the gap between ''synthetic-looking'' generative outputs and photorealistic reconstructions. Comprehensive benchmarks on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that current state-of-the-art methods often struggle to achieve geometric completeness and observational consistency simultaneously, especially under severe occlusions. In contrast, our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generative models and optimization-based frameworks in both geometric completeness and view-dependent appearance fidelity.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FlowObject**, a training-free framework that reformulates sparse-view 3D reconstruction as a **guided inverse problem** by steering the ODE trajectory.
- Employs a **dual-space guidance strategy** to balance learned generative priors with real-world observational evidence, mitigating 'synthetic bias'.
- Integrates a **3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) refinement stage** to bridge the gap between generative outputs and photorealistic reconstructions.
- Outperforms state-of-the-art models in **geometric completeness** and appearance fidelity, especially under severe occlusions.
key_results: Successfully bridges generative priors and reconstruction fidelity without requiring additional model training.
Significantly reduces synthetic bias where learned priors typically override observed instance evidence.
Outperforms existing optimization-based frameworks like 3DGS in hallucinating unobserved geometry.
Provides high-fidelity appearance through a secondary 3DGS refinement stage for photorealistic output.
Maintains strict consistency with real-world observations even in scenarios with severe image occlusions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: FlowObject researchers introduce a training-free flow-steering framework that combines generative priors with 3DGS for high-fidelity sparse-view reconstruction.

title: Sumi: Open Uniform Diffusion Language Model from Scratch
summary: Diffusion models have become a promising alternative to autoregressive models. Among these, uniform diffusion language models (UDLMs) permit any token to be updated at any step, in principle enabling more flexible generation. However, no UDLM has yet been pretrained from scratch at both large parameter scale and large token budget. Both autoregressive modeling and masked diffusion modeling already have capable models at scale that the community can study and build on; uniform diffusion has none. A scratch-pretrained UDLM at scale would provide a clean reference point for studying scaling behavior, generation dynamics, controllability, and trade-offs against established autoregressive and masked diffusion models. To this end, we introduce Sumi ("ink" in Japanese), a fully open 7B uniform diffusion language model pretrained from scratch on 1.5T tokens. Sumi performs competitively with autoregressive models trained at comparable token budgets on knowledge, reasoning, and coding benchmarks, while under-performing on commonsense benchmarks, where our education-heavy data mixture is a likely contributor. We release our model weights, checkpoints, and full training recipe, including a complete specification of the data mixture over publicly available corpora. We hope this release enables the community to study native uniform diffusion at scale and catalyzes work on its as-yet poorly understood aspects.

processed.summary:
- Introduction of **Sumi**, a 7B parameter **Uniform Diffusion Language Model (UDLM)** pretrained from scratch on **1.5 trillion tokens** to address the lack of large-scale open diffusion models.
- Employs a **native uniform diffusion** architecture that allows any token in a sequence to be updated at any step, offering more flexible generation compared to **autoregressive (AR)** models.
- Designed as a clean reference point for studying **scaling behavior**, generation dynamics, and controllability trade-offs against traditional transformer-based AR models.
- Released with full **training recipes**, data mixture specifications, and intermediate checkpoints to catalyze community research into non-autoregressive scaling.
key_results: Achieved competitive performance with AR models on knowledge, reasoning, and coding benchmarks.
Successfully scaled UDLM architecture to 7B parameters and 1.5T tokens from scratch.
Identified a performance gap in commonsense benchmarks, likely due to an education-heavy training data mixture.
Demonstrated that uniform diffusion is a viable architectural alternative for large-scale language modeling.
Provided the first open-source, large-scale UDLM baseline for the AI research community.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Contextual AI releases Sumi, a 7B uniform diffusion model, proving non-autoregressive models can match autoregressive performance at scale.

title: Spotlight: Synergizing Seed Exploration and Spot GPUs for DiT RL Post-Training
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) is prohibitively expensive, requiring thousands of high-end GPUs. Existing works explore two directions to reduce cost: seed exploration improves training convergence by selecting high-contrast samples, yet adds compute to the critical path; spot GPUs offer 69--77\% lower cost, yet sit idle during training because DiT rollouts finish nearly simultaneously, which prevents LLM-style pipelining of rollout with training. Spot preemptions further break Sequence Parallelism (SP) groups, fragmenting GPU topology.
  We present Spotlight, the first system that harvests spot GPUs for DiT RL post-training. Spotlight rests on two key insights we devise: (1)~we show that exploration can tolerate stale model weights because exploration that uses the model weights from the previous iteration preserves the relative ranking of random seeds, allowing exploration to run on idle spot GPUs during training. (2)~SP reconfiguration can reuse on-node state, reducing group recovery from minutes to sub-second launches. Built on these insights, Spotlight introduces three techniques: a bandit-based exploration planner that maximizes reward variance within the training time budget, elastic sequence parallelism that reconfigures SP groups on the fly via persistent schedulers and intra-node weight copying, and a preemption-aware pull-based request scheduler that balances load and commits in-flight state upon preemption. We implement Spotlight on the open-source RL platform ROLL and evaluate it on Qwen-Image post-training. Spotlight reaches the same target validation score $4\times$ faster than baselines, reducing total cost by $1.4$-$6.4\times$ while achieving superior image quality on DeepSeek-OCR and Geneval datasets with resolution $512\times512$ and $1280\times1280$.

processed.summary:
• Spotlight introduces a novel system for **DiT RL post-training** that harvests idle **spot GPUs** by decoupling seed exploration from the critical training path.
• The architecture leverages **Elastic Sequence Parallelism** (ESP) to reconfigure GPU groups in sub-seconds after preemptions, bypassing the minutes-long delays of traditional recovery.
• It utilizes a **bandit-based exploration planner** and the insight that seed exploration can tolerate **stale model weights**, allowing exploration to run asynchronously on low-cost spot instances.
• The system significantly optimizes training efficiency for high-resolution models like **Qwen-Image**, maintaining performance while drastically reducing infrastructure overhead.
key_results: Reaches target validation scores 4x faster than standard DiT RL baselines.
Reduces total compute costs by a factor of 1.4x to 6.4x using spot instances.
Achieves sub-second SP group recovery through on-node state reuse and persistent schedulers.
Maintains high image quality at resolutions up to 1280x1280 on DeepSeek-OCR benchmarks.
Successfully balances load via a preemption-aware pull-based request scheduler.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Spotlight, a system using spot GPUs and elastic parallelism to accelerate DiT RL post-training by 4x.

title: Enhancing Multilingual Reasoning via Steerable Model Merging
summary: Model merging is an effective technique for composing the capabilities of a multilingual model and a reasoning model. It has achieved promising generalization in multilingual reasoning tasks by aligning feature spaces of different models. However, the merged single model often fails to address the conflicts between source models, leading to suboptimal performance. In other words, the one-size-fits-all merging strategy may not align with the characteristics of different inputs which may require prioritizing certain models over others. To this end, we propose a Steerable Model Merging (ST-Merge) framework to modulate the contribution of each source model. To realize this idea, we introduce a gated cross-attention mechanism to weight or filter the two attended source models in an adaptive manner. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ST-Merge consistently outperforms multiple strong baselines on four multilingual reasoning benchmarks across 21 different languages.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **ST-Merge**, a framework that adaptively modulates contributions from **multilingual models** and **reasoning models** to resolve feature space conflicts.
* Introduces a **gated cross-attention mechanism** that dynamically weights or filters source model representations based on the specific characteristics of the input.
* Addresses the "one-size-fits-all" limitation of traditional **model merging** by enabling input-dependent steering of combined model capabilities.
* Demonstrates significant performance gains across 21 languages, validating the framework's effectiveness in **cross-lingual generalization**.
key_results: Consistent performance improvement over multiple strong model merging baselines.
Validated effectiveness across 4 major multilingual reasoning benchmarks.
Successful application to 21 diverse languages with varying linguistic features.
Effective resolution of conflicts between specialized source models via adaptive filtering.
Improved alignment of feature spaces compared to static or linear merging strategies.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ST-Merge, an adaptive framework that dynamically weights model contributions to enhance multilingual reasoning across 21 languages.

title: TRAP: Benchmark for Task-completion and Resistance to Active Privacy-extraction
summary: Agents are increasingly deployed in document-intensive workflows where sensitive private information is not an edge case but a routine input, e.g., an agent booking a flight needs passport numbers. In such settings, the agent must use private information to complete tasks accurately while never exposing it in its responses, because it cannot verify who is actually at the keyboard. These two obligations are in fundamental tension. A model capable enough to use private information for task completion can, by the same capability, be induced to reveal it. To evaluate the trade-off of task accuracy and privacy leakage, we introduce Task-completion and Resistance to Active Privacy-extraction (TRAP). Each scenario includes a document containing private information, a task query that requires the agent to invoke the correct tool using private fields, and an attack query that attempts to elicit the same information in natural language. Evaluating 22 models spanning frontier proprietary and open-source models at multiple scales, we find that all model families exhibit non-trivial leakage, and that instruction-following ability correlates with leakage rate. Existing prompt-based defenses reduce leakage but at significant cost to task accuracy. Prompt optimization fails to escape this trade-off. We demonstrate that this failure is not incidental. For any softmax-based model, no soft-constraint defense, e.g., prompt-based defenses, can jointly achieve high task success with zero leakage probability. Motivated by this impossibility result, we propose structural private field isolation, which replaces private fields with hash keys before they reach the model. This approach largely prevents leakage while keeping task accuracy.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **TRAP**, a benchmark evaluating the fundamental tension between an agent's ability to use **private sensitive data** for task completion and its resistance to **active extraction attacks**.
* Establishes a mathematical **impossibility result** for softmax-based models, proving that prompt-based "soft-constraints" cannot achieve zero leakage without sacrificing task utility.
* Analyzes 22 frontier and open-source models, revealing that superior **instruction-following capabilities** are positively correlated with higher privacy leakage rates.
* Proposes **structural private field isolation**, a novel architectural defense that replaces sensitive data with hash keys to maintain tool-use functionality while preventing natural language exposure.
key_results: Evaluated 22 models across frontier proprietary and open-source families.
Found that existing prompt-based defenses significantly reduce task accuracy while failing to stop leakage.
Observed that higher-performing models are more susceptible to clever privacy-extraction attacks.
Proven that softmax architectures cannot reach zero leakage via prompt optimization alone.
Demonstrated that field hashing effectively isolates private data without breaking agentic workflows.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Princeton and Virginia Tech researchers prove prompt-based privacy defenses fail and propose field hashing for secure AI agents.

title: G-IdiomAlign: A Gloss-Pivoted Benchmark for Cross-Lingual Idiom Alignment
summary: Idioms are difficult to transfer across languages due to their non-compositionality and weak surface-form grounding, making literal mappings unreliable. We present G-IdiomAlign, a gloss-pivoted benchmark where each idiom is anchored by an English gloss from Wiktionary. We further construct a high-confidence reference alignment set for reproducible evaluation. G-IdiomAlign supports two protocols: (1) a controlled Multiple-Choice Idiom Equivalence with typed distractors for error attribution; and (2) a Gloss-Contrastive Generation contrasting No-gloss and With-gloss inputs to isolate the effect of an explicit semantic pivot. Across diverse LLMs, a bias to literal translation is a dominant failure mode, especially when the target is a low-resource language. Glosses consistently improve Gloss-Contrastive Generation under an embedding-based semantic proxy, but performance remains modest, indicating substantial headroom in the open output space. Subsequent analysis on Qwen3-8B further suggests that cross-condition differences are concentrated more in attention heads than in layers, while better With-gloss generations coincide with stronger gloss anchoring.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **G-IdiomAlign**, a novel benchmark using English **gloss-pivoting** to solve the challenge of non-compositional idiom mapping across languages.
• Features a dual-protocol evaluation: **Multiple-Choice Idiom Equivalence** (MCIE) for error attribution and **Gloss-Contrastive Generation** (GCG) to measure semantic pivot effectiveness.
• Utilizes **attention head analysis** on models like Qwen3-8B to demonstrate that glosses improve translation by strengthening internal semantic anchoring rather than shifting layer-wise processing.
• Establishes a high-confidence **reference alignment set** from Wiktionary to enable reproducible evaluation of cross-lingual semantic reasoning in LLMs.
key_results: Literal translation bias is the primary failure mode for LLMs when dealing with idioms.
Explicit semantic glosses consistently improve translation quality across diverse model architectures.
Targeting low-resource languages significantly increases the rate of literal grounding errors.
Performance gains from gloss-anchoring are concentrated in specific attention heads rather than whole layers.
Significant performance headroom remains in the open output space, showing LLMs still struggle with idiomatic nuances.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce G-IdiomAlign, demonstrating that explicit semantic glosses significantly reduce literal translation errors in multilingual LLMs.

title: ThinkDeception: A Progressive Reinforcement Learning Framework for Interpretable Multimodal Deception Detection
summary: Multimodal deception detection is critical for identifying fraudulent intentions, yet existing approaches predominantly rely on end to end black--box paradigms. These methods suffer from a severe lack of interpretability failing to provide transparent reasoning trajectories and struggling to explicitly capture the subtle, cross modal inconsistencies inherent in deceptive behaviors. To transcend these limitations, we propose ThinkDeception, a novel and interpretable multimodal deception detection framework. As a pioneering effort, it introduces Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into this domain, transforming deception detection from a traditional binary classification task into an explicit cognitive reasoning process. Facilitated by the first meticulously annotated step--by--step multimodal Chain of Thought (CoT) dataset, we develop a foundational model, ThinkDeception Base, empirically validating the critical role of modal inconsistency in decoding deception. Building upon this foundation, our core innovation lies in proposing Visual-Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization(VAC--GRPO) equipped with a progressive training strategy. Distinct from standard GRPO, we stratify the training data into four progressive difficulty tiers, guiding the model through a psychologically grounded easy--to--hard cognitive transition. By innovatively coupling this dynamic curriculum scheduler with a multi dimensional, process aware reward mechanism and a reflective learning paradigm, we significantly elevate the model's overall reasoning quality. Extensive experiments on mainstream benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkDeception establishes a new SOTA, significantly outperforming existing methods in both detection accuracy and rationale quality. Ultimately, this work successfully drives the field of deception detection toward interpretable, multimodal cognitive reasoning.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **ThinkDeception**, a framework that transforms multimodal deception detection from a black-box binary classification into an **interpretable cognitive reasoning** process.
• Develops **ThinkDeception Base** using the first human-annotated **Multimodal Chain of Thought (CoT)** dataset specifically designed to capture cross-modal inconsistencies.
• Proposes **Visual-Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization (VAC-GRPO)**, which utilizes a **progressive training strategy** across four difficulty tiers to simulate human-like cognitive transitions.
• Implements a **multi-dimensional process-aware reward** mechanism and a reflective learning paradigm to significantly enhance the quality of generated rationales and detection accuracy.
key_results: Established a new State-Of-The-Art (SOTA) on mainstream multimodal deception detection benchmarks.
Demonstrated that progressive curriculum learning (easy-to-hard) in RL leads to more stable and higher-quality reasoning trajectories.
Proved that cross-modal inconsistencies (audio vs. visual) are the primary indicators for decoding deceptive behavior in MLLMs.
Improved both quantitative detection accuracy and qualitative rationale interpretability compared to traditional end-to-end models.
Validated the effectiveness of VAC-GRPO over standard GRPO for tasks requiring fine-grained multimodal alignment.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ThinkDeception, using progressive GRPO to turn multimodal deception detection into an interpretable reasoning process.

title: Beyond Tokenization: Direct Timestep Embedding and Contrastive Alignment for Time-Series Question Answering
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have given rise to time-series question answering (TSQA), which formulates time-series analysis as natural-language question answering. However, directly feeding raw numerical series into LLMs suffers from a tokenization bottleneck: Byte Pair Encoding fragments continuous values into unstable tokens whose embeddings lack meaningful metric structure, resulting in the loss of magnitude, scale, and trend information. Prior methods use patch-based encoders that split the series into fixed windows, locking in one granularity that breaks patterns and hides exact timesteps, through a separate module that rarely transfers across datasets with different lengths or sampling rates. To address this challenge, we propose CADE (Contrastive Alignment with Direct Embedding), a novel framework for TSQA built upon two key components: direct timestep embedding and semantic alignment. The proposed framework maps each timestep directly into the LLM embedding space through a point-wise linear encoder and MLP projector, preserving exact index-level access while eliminating the need for patching and padding. To further bridge the semantic gap between time-series and language representations, we introduce a novel one-directional supervised contrastive loss that aligns time-series embeddings with frozen class-name text anchors. Experimental results on the public Time-MQA benchmark demonstrate that our framework consistently improves performance across six TSQA tasks, outperforming both open-source and proprietary LLM baselines.

processed.summary:
Proposes **CADE (Contrastive Alignment with Direct Embedding)** to overcome the **tokenization bottleneck** in Time-Series Question Answering by mapping raw numerical data directly into the LLM embedding space.
Utilizes a **point-wise linear encoder** and MLP projector to preserve exact index-level information, eliminating the need for traditional patching or padding which often breaks temporal patterns.
Introduces a **one-directional supervised contrastive loss** that aligns time-series representations with frozen text anchors to bridge the semantic gap between modalities.
Demonstrates that eliminating **Byte Pair Encoding (BPE)** fragmentation preserves magnitude, scale, and trend information critical for accurate temporal reasoning.
key_results: Overcomes the inherent instability and fragmentation of BPE for numerical data.
Preserves exact index-level access for higher-resolution temporal analysis.
Outperforms both open-source and proprietary LLM baselines on the Time-MQA benchmark.
Eliminates the requirement for fixed-window patch-based encoders that limit dataset transferability.
Successfully aligns time-series features with natural language via semantic text anchors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop CADE, a framework using direct embeddings to solve the tokenization bottleneck in time-series question answering.

title: CAPRA: Scaling Feedback on Software Architecture Deliverables with a Multi-Agent LLM System
summary: Automated assessment in software engineering education has advanced significantly for code grading and essay scoring. However, reviewing software architecture deliverables, which requires analyzing structural completeness and requirements traceability, has not yet been fully automated. Applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to this task requires robust architectures to ensure technical feedback is accurate and reliable for students. This paper presents CAPRA (Configurable Architecture Proficiency Report Assessment), a multi-agent LLM system that analyzes software architecture deliverables to generate personalized, template-compliant LaTeX feedback. As a core design choice, CAPRA coordinates multiple specialized agents and employs a Python-based microservice for multi-modal document extraction, utilizing PyMuPDF and vision-enabled LLMs (specifically gpt-4o) to parse text and UML diagrams. To ensure educational reliability and mitigate hallucinations, CAPRA introduces a deterministic Evidence Anchoring step using fuzzy matching via normalized Levenshtein distance, along with a ConsistencyManager agent that cross-verifies, deduplicates, and merges findings. System performance is assessed using a structured eight-criterion binary evaluation taxonomy covering: (i) extraction completeness, (ii) feature validation, (iii) issue grounding and severity detection, (iv) recommendation specificity and traceability, and (v) template and tone compliance. A preliminary empirical evaluation on 10 student reports shows that CAPRA satisfied 88.8% of the evaluated criteria under a strict two-rater aggregation rule, achieved moderate inter-rater agreement with human evaluators (kappa = 0.582), and processed each report in slightly over 4 minutes. While these results support the viability of LLM-supported architectural feedback, human oversight remains essential for subjective assessment dimensions.

processed.summary:
- **CAPRA Framework**: A specialized multi-agent LLM system designed to automate the evaluation of software architecture deliverables, integrating **multimodal extraction** for text and UML diagrams.
- **Technical Architecture**: Employs a Python-based microservice utilizing **PyMuPDF** and **gpt-4o** for parsing, alongside a deterministic **Evidence Anchoring** step using normalized Levenshtein distance to prevent hallucinations.
- **Agentic Coordination**: Uses a **ConsistencyManager** agent to cross-verify, deduplicate, and merge findings from specialized sub-agents into personalized, template-compliant **LaTeX feedback**.
- **Evaluation Rigor**: Introduces an eight-criterion binary taxonomy focusing on **extraction completeness**, feature validation, and issue grounding to ensure educational reliability.
key_results: Satisfied 88.8% of the evaluated criteria across 10 student reports under strict rater aggregation.
Achieved moderate inter-rater agreement with human experts (kappa = 0.582).
Processed complex architectural reports in an average of 4 minutes per document.
Successfully implemented fuzzy matching for grounding LLM claims in document evidence.
Demonstrated high adherence to specific educational templates and tone requirements.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CAPRA researchers develop a multi-agent system using multimodal LLMs to provide automated, evidence-anchored feedback on software architecture reports.

title: Visual-OPSD: Cross-Modal On-Policy Self-Distillation for Efficient Unified Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) interleave generated ''visual thoughts'' (VTs) with text reasoning to improve spatial tasks. This incurs roughly an order-of-magnitude inference cost from multi-step diffusion. We find this cost yields limited direct benefit. On ThinkMorph, removing or noising VTs barely changes accuracy across nine benchmarks. Once rendered, attention concentrates on the VT regardless of content. Yet a KL diagnostic shows that conditioning on a privileged VT trace shifts the model's completion distribution. This suggests the generation pathway encodes useful reasoning beyond the rendered pixels. Motivated by this gap, we propose Visual On-Policy Self-Distillation(Visual-OPSD). Teacher and student share identical weights but differ in context: the teacher sees privileged VTs while the student sees only the question. Token-level JSD distillation on on-policy student trajectories transfers the teacher's reasoning to a text-only student. Across nine benchmarks, Visual-OPSD improves over its generative teacher by $+3.40$pp with $14.3\times$ speedup (10.0s vs. 142.8s per sample) and outperforms same-scale VLMs by $+63.83$pp on VSP. A Gaussian-noise control ($+0.40$pp vs. $+10.28$pp for real VTs) and $58.4\%$ closure of the KL gap confirm that gains come from the semantic content of the generation pathway.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Visual-OPSD**, a cross-modal self-distillation framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from "visual thoughts" (VTs) into text-only completions without expensive diffusion rendering.
- Discovers that the semantic content of the **generation pathway** in Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) provides more value than the actual rendered pixels, which were found to be redundant.
- Utilizes **Token-level Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD)** on on-policy student trajectories to align a text-only student with a teacher model that has access to privileged visual contexts.
- Bypasses the high latency of multi-step diffusion, resulting in an architecture that is significantly more efficient while maintaining high-fidelity multimodal reasoning.
key_results: Achieved a 14.3x inference speedup, reducing per-sample time from 142.8s to 10.0s.
Improved accuracy by +3.40 percentage points over the original generative teacher model.
Outperformed same-scale Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by +63.83 percentage points on the Visual Spatial Perception (VSP) benchmark.
Closed the KL divergence gap between student and teacher distributions by 58.4%.
Verified via Gaussian-noise controls that gains result from semantic reasoning content rather than simple noise augmentation (+10.28pp vs +0.40pp).
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Visual-OPSD to distill expensive visual-reasoning traces into text-only models for 14x faster, more accurate inference.

title: EfficientRollout: System-Aware Self-Speculative Decoding for RL Rollouts
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a representative post-training paradigm for LLMs, enabling strong reasoning and agentic capabilities. However, rollout generation remains a dominant latency bottleneck because autoregressive sampling decodes responses sequentially and a small number of long-tailed generations often determine completion time. Speculative decoding (SD) offers a natural way to address this bottleneck, as it is a well-established technique for serving fixed LLMs that reduces latency by rapidly drafting tokens and accepting them through parallel verification while preserving the target-model distribution. However, its practical speedups do not directly carry over to RL rollouts: (i) the evolving target policy makes any fixed drafter increasingly mismatched with the policy's output distribution; and (ii) active batch sizes shrink throughout rollout decoding, shifting decoding from compute-bound to memory-bound regimes where parallel verification can exploit underutilized compute. Therefore, accelerating RL rollouts requires both a drafter that remains effective under long, high-temperature generations from an evolving policy and system-aware use of SD that avoids compute-bound regimes. We present EfficientRollout, a system-aware self-SD framework designed to address this gap for RL rollouts. EfficientRollout induces a quantized drafter from the target model (i.e. self-speculative decoding), keeping it coupled to the evolving policy without separate drafter pretraining or online adaptation. It further coordinates a system-aware SD toggle policy with acceptance-aware draft-length adaptation, enabling speculation only in beneficial regimes while matching the drafting budget to evolving drafter quality. EfficientRollout reduces rollout and end-to-end latency by up to 19.6% and 12.7%, respectively, over an accelerated AR rollout baseline, while preserving final model quality.

processed.summary:
* **EfficientRollout** introduces a system-aware **self-speculative decoding** framework designed specifically to accelerate the **RL rollout** phase in LLM training.
* It employs a **quantized drafter** derived from the target model itself, ensuring the drafter remains aligned with the **evolving policy** without the need for separate pretraining.
* The framework features a **system-aware toggle policy** and **acceptance-aware adaptation** to ensure speculation only occurs during memory-bound regimes where compute is underutilized.
* By dynamically adjusting draft lengths based on real-time performance, it maintains efficiency even during long, **high-temperature generations** common in agentic reasoning tasks.
key_results: Reduces rollout latency by up to 19.6% compared to accelerated autoregressive baselines.
Decreases total end-to-end RL training latency by up to 12.7%.
Preserves the target-model distribution and final model quality throughout the training process.
Eliminates the mismatch between fixed drafters and evolving RL policies via self-speculation.
Optimizes hardware utilization by transitioning between speculative and standard decoding as batch sizes shrink.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce EfficientRollout to accelerate RL training using system-aware self-speculative decoding and quantized model drafters.

title: Convergence of Replicator Dynamics in the Repeated Prisoner's Dilemma with Restarts
summary: We investigate a population of self-interested agents playing a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma under the trigger-restart mechanism. Under such a mechanism, agents play a sequence of symmetric games with their partner, and restart the interaction if their actions disagree. Our work focuses on the convergence of replicator dynamics in a well-mixed population of agents, where the emergence of cooperation is challenged by the individual incentive for exploitation. Formulating the corresponding parametrised normal-form game, with agents each adopting a length-m strategy sequence, we show that increasing the strategy length enables cooperation to emerge and stabilise. We provide exact convergence guarantees for restricted strategy lengths and, in the general payoff configuration, provide the necessary parametric conditions for the stability of cooperative strategies. By deriving an exact formula for the number of stable sequences, we find structural properties necessary for stability, as agents must learn to initially defect - the so-called "hazing period" - before cooperating indefinitely. Our analysis shows that, while optimal cooperative sequences exist, agents favour less-optimal sequences with a longer hazing period, which possess larger basins of attraction.

processed.summary:
- Analyzes **replicator dynamics** within a population of self-interested agents playing a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma via a **trigger-restart mechanism**.
- Shows that **strategy sequence length** is a critical parameter, where longer sequences provide the necessary complexity for cooperation to emerge and stabilize.
- Identifies the **hazing period**—a phase of initial defection—as a mandatory structural property for achieving long-term cooperative stability.
- Demonstrates a trade-off where agents favor sequences with **larger basins of attraction** over those that are mathematically optimal for payoffs.
key_results: Increased strategy length enables cooperation to emerge and stabilize in well-mixed populations.
Exact convergence guarantees are provided for restricted strategy lengths.
Stability of cooperative strategies requires specific parametric conditions in general payoff configurations.
A structural 'hazing period' of initial defection is necessary for a sequence to be stable.
Agents naturally favor less-optimal cooperative sequences because they possess larger basins of attraction.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that longer strategy sequences and initial 'hazing' periods enable stable cooperation in multi-agent populations.

title: Online Reward-Punishment Learning from Fixed-Channel Perceptual Event Streams without Environment Rewards
summary:
We study online reward-punishment learning when the environment provides no scalar reward or evaluative label. At each step the agent receives only a fixed-channel perceptual packet, and quantities such as pain, energy, contact, damage, or cognitive error are treated as perceptual dimensions whose valence must be inferred from transition consequences. OHIRL separates four roles: M_psi learns next-packet prediction, D_omega models residual dynamics, C_eta is a fixed internal post-transition trajectory evaluator, and B_xi learns to use the resulting value evidence for later policy updates and action scoring. C_eta uses a recovery-positive and persistence/growth-negative residual-regulation orientation; a coefficient-origin audit shows that equal-unit, raw-equal, and random monotone variants preserve more than 92% of the released top-action rankings, while sign inversion preserves 0%.
  The reward-free protocol exposes observation transitions while withholding environment rewards, delayed external evaluators, success labels, and action-goodness labels. A conditional error decomposition separates B_xi evidence-estimation error from residual policy-optimization error. In a 2x2-XOR packet task, medicine and chili acquire opposite value under visual XOR contexts, and the same pain or spice increase can be positive or negative depending on consequence structure; B_xi reaches 0.952 balanced reward-sign accuracy. In a full online-interleaved audit, M_psi reaches holdout R2=0.907, B_xi reaches 0.940 sign accuracy, and the policy reaches 0.979 optimal-action accuracy, while immediate packet scores, prediction-error rewards, shuffled targets, zero reward, and error-reduction controls collapse. Hidden-reward CartPole and Taxi controls, public-context no-leakage audits, and module-role ablations further test information boundaries and component necessity.

processed.summary:
• The **OHIRL framework** (Online Hidden-Reward Learning) decouples agent functions into four roles: next-packet prediction, residual dynamics modeling, trajectory evaluation, and value estimation.
• It introduces a method for agents to infer **reward-punishment valence** from raw perceptual dimensions (e.g., pain, energy, damage) based on transition consequences rather than external labels.
• The architecture employs a **recovery-positive and persistence-negative** residual-regulation orientation to generate internal value evidence for policy updates.
• A **conditional error decomposition** is utilized to separate estimation errors in value evidence from residual errors in policy optimization, enhancing evaluation granularity.
key_results: B_xi module achieved 0.952 balanced reward-sign accuracy in complex XOR-based perceptual tasks.
Policy reached 0.979 optimal-action accuracy without any external success labels or environment rewards.
M_psi prediction module achieved a holdout R2 of 0.907 during online-interleaved audits.
Internal evaluator variants (equal-unit/random monotone) preserved over 92% of top-action rankings, while sign inversion reached 0%.
Successfully demonstrated autonomous reward inference and task completion in hidden-reward CartPole and Taxi benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce OHIRL to enable agents to learn reward-punishment structures directly from raw perceptual streams without external feedback.

title: GraphPO: Graph-based Policy Optimization for Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a standard paradigm for enhancing the capability of large reasoning models. RLVR typically samples responses independently and optimizes the policy using from final answers. This paradigm has two limitations. First, independently responses often contain similar intermediate reasoning steps, causing redundant exploration and wasted computation. Second, sparse final-answer rewards make it hard to identify useful steps. Tree-based methods partly address this problem by sharing prefixes and comparing branches from the same prefix to provide fine-grained signals. However, tree branches are still expanded independently. When different branches reach similar reasoning states, they cannot share information and repeat similar exploration. Moreover, tree-based methods ignore such dispersion and only perform local comparisons within separate branches, which can lead to higher variance in advantage estimation. To address this challenge, we propose GraphPO (Graph-based Policy Optimization), a novel RL framework that represents rollouts as a directed acyclic graph, with reasoning steps as edges and semantic states summarized from the reasoning paths as nodes. GraphPO merges semantically equivalent reasoning paths into equivalence classes, allowing them to share suffixes and reallocating budget away from redundant expansions to diverse exploration. Furthermore, we assign efficiency advantages to incoming edges and correctness advantages to outgoing edges, thereby improving inference efficiency while deriving process supervision from outcome. Theory shows that GraphPO reduces advantage-estimation variance and enhances reasoning efficiency. Experiments on three LLMs across reasoning and agentic search benchmarks show that GraphPO consistently outperforms chain- and tree-based baselines with the same token budgets or response budgets.

processed.summary:
- GraphPO replaces traditional chain and tree-based rollouts with a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** structure where reasoning steps are edges and semantic states are nodes.
- The framework merges **semantically equivalent reasoning paths**, allowing the model to share suffixes and reallocate computation from redundant steps to diverse exploration.
- It implements a dual-advantage system, assigning **efficiency advantages** to incoming edges and **correctness advantages** to outgoing edges to derive process supervision from outcome rewards.
- Theoretical analysis confirms that this graph-based approach reduces **advantage-estimation variance** and enhances the efficiency of reasoning model training.
key_results: GraphPO consistently outperforms chain- and tree-based baselines across three different LLMs.
The method significantly reduces redundant exploration by sharing suffix computation between similar states.
Improved performance was observed on both complex mathematical reasoning and agentic search benchmarks.
Reduces advantage-estimation variance compared to tree-based methods that rely on local branch comparisons.
Maintains superior accuracy under identical token or response budgets compared to standard RLVR methods.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce GraphPO, a DAG-based RL framework that merges equivalent reasoning paths to optimize exploration and model reasoning.

title: RTSGameBench: An RTS Benchmark for Strategic Reasoning by Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle with strategic reasoning, i.e., anticipating and influencing other agents' actions, under uncertainty in competitive and cooperative settings. Real-time strategy (RTS) games can be a natural testbed for diagnosing this limitation, as they demand coordination with allies, adaptation to opponents' strategy, and long-horizon planning under partial observability. However, existing RTS benchmarks offer limited evaluation scope, lack systematic competency diagnosis, and remain fixed in the pre-designed scenario coverage. To address these limitations, we present RTSGameBench, which is built on Beyond All Reason, a large-scale RTS game with an expanded battlefield that demands broader strategy diversity than the existing testbeds. The proposed benchmark provides evaluations through diverse gameplay across various matchup structures, diagnostic assessment via mini-games, each targeting an individual strategic competency, and extensible coverage via a self-evolving generation framework that converts free-form queries into new mini-games, improving over successive cycles. Additionally, for VLMs to operate in large-scale RTS games, we provide RTSGameAgent that manages units by an FSM with agentic memory. We empirically validate that multiple state-of-the-art VLMs do not perform well when matchups demand tighter coordination, multiagent coordination and when task scale increases.

processed.summary:
• **RTSGameBench** is a new benchmark leveraging the 'Beyond All Reason' engine to assess **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** on strategic reasoning and partial observability.
• It features a **self-evolving generation framework** that programmatically translates free-form queries into diverse, diagnostic mini-games for extensible evaluation.
• The architecture introduces **RTSGameAgent**, which utilizes a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** and agentic memory to bridge high-level VLM strategic decisions with low-level unit management.
• Empirical evaluations reveal that current SOTA VLMs exhibit significant performance degradation in **multi-agent coordination** and large-scale combat scenarios.
key_results: SOTA VLMs show significant performance drops in scenarios requiring tight multi-agent coordination.
Strategic reasoning capabilities scale poorly as battlefield size and unit complexity increase.
The benchmark successfully identifies specific diagnostic failures in individual strategic competencies.
The self-evolving generation framework provides a scalable method for creating diverse RTS evaluation scenarios.
The FSM-based agentic memory architecture is essential for VLMs to operate effectively in real-time strategy environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers release RTSGameBench, a strategic reasoning benchmark revealing that VLMs struggle with complex multi-agent coordination and scaling.

title: Decoupling Search from Reasoning: A Vendor-Agnostic Grounding Architecture for LLM Agents
summary: Production LLM agents increasingly depend on real-time search, yet native search grounding bundles retrieval policy, provider choice, evidence injection, cost, latency, and generation behavior behind a single model-provider boundary. This coupling makes grounding hard to inspect, tune, reuse, or port, and can trigger Search-Induced Verbosity that breaks strict output contracts. We present Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG), a vendor-agnostic boundary that moves grounding outside the reasoning model through an MCP-compatible gateway, exposing provider routing, source-aware context rendering, configured fallback, retrieval-depth control, and exact plus semantic caching as first-class controls. Across five frontier models on SimpleQA, FreshQA, and HotpotQA, native search leads on recency-sensitive FreshQA, but DSG exposes a stronger frontier when control matters: on SimpleQA it nearly matches native accuracy (86.1% vs. 87.7%) at 91% lower search cost, preserves concise answer contracts, and reaches a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate with 68% lower latency. Deployed as a shared production grounding layer for large-scale agentic workloads with interchangeable models, DSG matches or slightly exceeds native-search accuracy on an e-commerce query-understanding (QIU) workload while cutting search cost by over 98%. Real-time grounding is best treated as an optimizable interface boundary, not a fixed model feature.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG)**, a vendor-agnostic architecture that moves retrieval logic outside the reasoning model via an **MCP-compatible gateway**.
* Provides granular control over **grounding parameters**, including provider routing, retrieval depth, and source-aware context rendering to mitigate **Search-Induced Verbosity**.
* Implements **exact and semantic caching** as first-class controls to drastically reduce latency and operational costs for large-scale **agentic workloads**.
key_results: Achieved 86.1% accuracy on SimpleQA, nearly matching native model performance of 87.7%.
Reduced search costs by 91% on benchmark datasets and over 98% on production e-commerce workloads.
Realized 68% lower latency through a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate.
Maintained strict output format contracts by decoupling retrieval from the core generation policy.
Demonstrated successful vendor-agnostic implementation across five different frontier LLMs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DSG to decouple search from reasoning, cutting agent costs by 98% through an MCP-compatible gateway.

title: SenFlow: Inter-Sentence Flow Modeling for AI-Generated Text Detection in Hybrid Documents
summary: Sentence-level AI-generated text detection (S-AGTD) for hybrid documents, where humans and LLMs co-author one text, faces two gaps: existing methods classify each sentence in isolation, discarding inter-sentence dependencies, and existing benchmarks omit the newest generation of generators. We construct MOSAIC, a benchmark of 16,000 hybrid documents over PubMed and XSum, generated by DeepSeek-V3.2 and Kimi K2 under stringent quality controls including a perplexity-consistency filter absent from prior benchmarks. We recast S-AGTD as structured prediction over the document sentence sequence and instantiate it as SenFlow, integrating graph-based inter-sentence propagation with linear-chain CRF decoding in a single document-level pass over a sentence graph. SenFlow reaches state-of-the-art performance on MOSAIC, with a +4.15 pp average Macro-F1 margin on cross-domain transfer, the hardest of three protocols of increasing difficulty. We further find that even after the perplexity filter equalizes overt cues, AI insertions retain a generator-dependent sentence-length gap that sentence-level detectors still exploit. Code and data: https://github.com/luojingkun22/SenFlow

processed.summary:
- Introduces **SenFlow**, a novel framework for **Sentence-level AI-Generated Text Detection (S-AGTD)** that treats hybrid document analysis as a structured prediction task.
- Implements a unique architecture combining **graph-based inter-sentence propagation** with **linear-chain CRF decoding** to capture structural dependencies across human-AI transitions.
- Contributes the **MOSAIC benchmark**, which addresses the gap in current datasets by using 16,000 documents generated by advanced models like **DeepSeek-V3.2** and **Kimi K2**.
- Features a single-pass document-level inference over a sentence graph, optimizing both **computational efficiency** and detection accuracy in co-authored texts.
key_results: SenFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MOSAIC benchmark for sentence-level detection.
The model demonstrates a +4.15 percentage point Macro-F1 margin on cross-domain transfer tasks.
The MOSAIC benchmark introduces a perplexity-consistency filter to ensure high-quality, human-like AI generation.
Evidence shows that AI-generated segments retain a distinct, generator-dependent sentence-length gap even when perplexity is equalized.
Integrated CRF decoding significantly outperforms traditional methods that classify sentences in isolation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Luo et al. introduce SenFlow to detect AI-generated sentences in hybrid documents by modeling inter-sentence flow and structural dependencies.

title: SciRisk-Bench: A Risk-Dimension-Aware Benchmark for AI4Science Safety
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in AI for Science (AI4Science) workflows, from scientific question answering and literature analysis to laboratory planning and autonomous discovery. This progress creates an urgent need for safety benchmarks that evaluate not only scientific competence, but also whether models recognize and avoid risks in high-stakes scientific contexts. Existing AI4Science safety datasets cover several disciplines and task formats, leaving the underlying risk dimensions underspecified. We introduce \textbf{SciRisk-Bench}, a benchmark designed to evaluate AI4Science safety from two complementary perspectives: explicit risk dimensions and scientific disciplines. SciRisk-Bench covers 7 disciplines, 31 subdisciplines and 10 risk dimensions. In the experimental section, we evaluate both mainstream LLMs and science-oriented LLMs across risk dimensions, disciplines, and sub-disciplines, enabling fine-grained diagnosis of where scientific models remain unsafe.

processed.summary:
• **SciRisk-Bench** introduces a novel evaluation framework designed to measure the safety of **AI4Science** models in high-stakes laboratory and research contexts.
• The architecture employs a dual-perspective taxonomy focusing on **10 explicit risk dimensions** and **7 scientific disciplines** to identify specific model failure points.
• The framework enables a fine-grained diagnosis of **mainstream and science-oriented LLMs**, highlighting where scientific competence lacks necessary safety guardrails.
key_results: Identifies 10 specific risk dimensions for AI in scientific discovery.
Spans 7 major disciplines and 31 subdisciplines for comprehensive coverage.
Benchmarks both general-purpose and specialized scientific LLMs.
Reveals significant safety vulnerabilities in current science-oriented models.
Provides a standardized methodology for evaluating risk-aware scientific model behavior.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SciRisk-Bench to evaluate AI4Science safety across ten risk dimensions and seven scientific disciplines.
